description: This is minified third-party vendor code (dat.GUI) where `c` is a hardcoded property name from internal call sites (e.g. "r", "g", "b", "h", "s", "v"), not attacker-controlled input; the bracket assignment `b.__state[c]=...` is a color-space conversion, not a prototype pollution sink reachable from untrusted data.

The regex /([a-z]+)(\d+)/ has no nested/overlapping quantifiers or ambiguous alternation, so it cannot exhibit catastrophic backtracking; it matches in linear time regardless of input. This is a bundled third-party library (three.js) and the pattern itself is not vulnerable, so the ReDoS classification is a mis-detection.

This is vendored three.js animation code indexing morphTargetInfluences with a numeric frame counter (animation.lastFrame), not an attacker-controlled key string like '__proto__'/'constructor'/'prototype'; there is no dynamic object property assignment here, so the prototype-pollution classification is a mistake by the tool.

This is data seeding code (challenge/data creator) where `challenge.key` comes from static internal challenge definitions bulk-inserted into the DB, not from untrusted user input; the bracket assignment is a generic pattern match, not exploitable prototype pollution.

User-controlled req.query.q is only length-truncated (no escaping/parameterization) and is directly string-concatenated into a raw SQL query executed via sequelize.query, allowing injection e.g. via a crafted 'q' value containing quote characters.

Before reaching the sink, criteria is rejected if it contains any quote character, semicolon, '#', or 'and'/'or' (case-insensitive), which blocks the string-literal breakout needed for injection; the literal '%' padding around ${criteria} in the query also prevents a trailing-backslash escape of the closing quote. The taint tool flags the sink without recognizing this blacklist as an effective sanitizer for this specific query shape.

req.body.email and req.body.password are concatenated directly into a raw SQL string passed to sequelize.query; the preceding regex check is a bypassable blacklist and doesn't even halt execution (no return after res.send), so the tainted query still runs regardless, making this exploitable SQL injection.

The email parameter is safely bound via `$1`/`bind`, and the password is passed through `security.hash()` (SHA-256 hex digest) before string interpolation, so the interpolated value can only contain hex characters and cannot break out of the quoted string to inject SQL.

req.body.email is concatenated directly into a raw SQL string; the regex check on line 15 is trivially bypassable (blocks only ['-;] literally) and even when it matches, execution falls through to the query anyway since there's no return/else after res.status(451).send(). This is the well-known Juice Shop login SQL injection challenge and is exploitable.

The email parameter (req.body.email) is passed via Sequelize's parameterized `replacements`, not string-concatenated, so it cannot inject SQL. The password value is first passed through `security.hash()`, and typical hash functions produce fixed-format hex output with no SQL metacharacters, so the template-literal interpolation of the hash result is not attacker-controllable in a way that enables injection.

req.body.email is directly interpolated into a raw SQL string via template literal with no escaping/parameterization before being passed to sequelize.query, allowing classic SQL injection (e.g., login bypass via ' OR '1'='1); this is the known intentionally-vulnerable Juice Shop login pattern.

req.body.email and req.body.password are directly interpolated into a raw SQL string passed to sequelize.query without parameterization or escaping, allowing classic authentication-bypass/UNION-based SQL injection (e.g. email = ' OR '1'='1).

req.body.email and req.body.password are directly interpolated into a raw SQL string via template literals; the preceding regex check on line 15 only sends a 451 response without a return/next(), so execution falls through to the vulnerable query regardless, and even if it did block, blacklist-style quote/dash/semicolon matching is trivially bypassable.

User-controlled `criteria` from req.query.q is interpolated directly into a raw SQL string via template literal; the `.replace()` call on line 5 doesn't mutate the string in place and its result is discarded, so no sanitization actually occurs before the tainted value reaches the sequelize.query sink.

The guard on line 6 uses `!startsWith('apple') || !startsWith('orange')`, which by De Morgan's law is equivalent to `!(startsWith('apple') && startsWith('orange'))` — since no string can start with both prefixes simultaneously, this condition is always true, so the function always returns 400 before line 10 executes. The tainted 'criteria' therefore never reaches the sql sink for any input, making the reported flow unreachable despite the underlying string-interpolation pattern being a latent SQ

Assigning to quantityMap[item.ProductId] only sets a property (or at worst __proto__) on this single local component instance object, not a shared/global object merged via a generic deep-merge; it does not pollute Object.prototype or any object reachable elsewhere, so it's not exploitable prototype pollution.

This is a Jasmine/Jest unit test assigning a dummy placeholder string ('test-private-key') to a component property to exercise error-handling logic; it is test fixture data, not a real hardcoded cryptographic key or secret with any production exposure.

The pattern `^(?=.*?[...])` contains a single unnested quantifier (`.*?`) followed by one character class with no overlapping/ambiguous alternation, so matching is linear in input length and cannot exhibit catastrophic backtracking regardless of input; it's also applied to a password field bounded in practice.
This is a value assigned in an Angular unit test to verify client-side password-match form validation logic, not a credential used for authentication or stored anywhere sensitive.

This is test/mock code in an Angular spec file assigning a mock socket event handler function; there is no dynamic property assignment from untrusted input or merge/clone operation that could pollute Object.prototype.

The `phase` key is statically typed as `Phase` (a fixed union like 'find it' | 'fix it'), not an arbitrary attacker-controlled string, so `solves[challengeKey][phase] = verdict` cannot be used to set `__proto__` or other polluting keys; this is a generic bracket-assignment pattern match, not exploitable prototype pollution.

The regex /[/#]{0,2} vuln-code-snippet start (?<challenges>.*)/g has no nested/overlapping quantifiers or ambiguous alternation; {0,2} is a small bounded repetition and .* is a single linear scan, so it cannot exhibit catastrophic backtracking regardless of input size or content.

jwt.verify() is called on user-controlled token without restricting allowed algorithms, so an attacker who knows the RSA public key can forge a token signed with HS256 using that key as the HMAC secret, causing decoded (attacker-controlled) data to be trusted and cached as an authenticated user identity — a classic JWT algorithm-confusion authorization bypass.

This is a simple object property assignment (obj[key] = value) on server-controlled data (a JWT token and a database-generated user id), not a recursive merge of attacker-supplied JSON into an object — the actual prototype-pollution pattern. Even if token were '__proto__', it would only alter tokenMap's own prototype, not Object.prototype globally, so there's no genuine cross-object pollution primitive here.

The shown code only returns a cached HMAC key sourced from an environment variable or local file and is consumed by crypto.createHmac in ctfFlag; there is no HTML sink or user-controlled input in this snippet, so the 'flows to html sink' claim does not match the code shown.

The regex /(\r\n|\n|\r)/gm has no nested quantifiers or overlapping alternations that can cause catastrophic backtracking; each alternative matches a fixed, non-overlapping 1-2 character sequence, so matching is linear in input length regardless of content.

The value is JSON.stringify-encoded and written as an SSE data frame (res.write with `data: ...\n\n`), not inserted into an HTML document or response with an HTML content-type; JSON.stringify escapes quotes/control chars appropriately for this sink, so there's no server-side HTML injection here regardless of what a client does with the delta text.

The sink at line 18 is a lookup into an in-memory Map (security.authenticatedUsers.get(...)), not any network/HTTP request; there is no outbound request constructed from the cookie value, so this cannot be SSRF regardless of taint from req.cookies.

baseUser[field] is a single-level bracket assignment on a plain object where field is a bare string from splitting a query param (no dotted/nested path), so even if field were '__proto__' or 'constructor' it can only rebind baseUser's own prototype/property locally—it never traverses into a nested object or merges attacker-controlled values into Object.prototype, so there's no global pollution vector here.

req.cookies.token is only used as a lookup key into an in-memory Map (security.authenticatedUsers.get), not passed to any network/URL-fetching sink, so this is a rule mis-classification with no actual SSRF.

req.body.email and req.body.password are directly interpolated into a raw SQL string passed to sequelize.query with no parameterization or escaping, allowing classic SQL injection (e.g. auth bypass via ' OR '1'='1).

The regex /(?<major>\d+).(?<minor>\d+).(?<patch>\d+)/ has no nested/overlapping quantifiers so it cannot exhibit catastrophic backtracking, and its input is utils.version() — an internal, non-attacker-controlled string — not user input, so it isn't reachable as a DoS vector.

BasketModel is a Sequelize (SQL) model, not a NoSQL store, and req.params.id from Express routing is always a plain string (unlike req.query/req.body which can be parsed into nested objects), so it cannot be crafted into an operator-injection object like {$ne: null}; Sequelize will safely parameterize the equality check.

req.cookies.token is used only as a lookup key into an in-memory Map (security.authenticatedUsers.get()) to retrieve the current session's user; it is never used to construct a URL or make an outbound request, so there is no SSRF sink here at all — a clear mis-classification by the tool.
The redirect target is server-controlled (process.env.BASE_PATH), not user-controllable request data, so there is no attacker-influenced input flowing to the redirect sink despite the taint label.

process.env.BASE_PATH is a server-side configuration value set by the deployment operator, not attacker-controlled input, so there is no untrusted taint reaching the redirect sink despite the tool's generic tainting of process.env.

Line 21 only uses req.cookies.token as a lookup key into an in-memory Map (security.authenticatedUsers.get), not as input to the fetch() SSRF sink; the actual sink at line 24 uses `url` derived from req.body.imageUrl, an unrelated taint source. The tool appears to have mis-attributed the sink location/taint source.

The file path uses loggedInUser.data.id (from an authenticated session lookup, not the tainted url) and ext, which is constrained to a hardcoded whitelist ['jpg','jpeg','png','svg','gif'] with a safe default — no attacker-controlled path segments reach the fs.createWriteStream call, so path traversal isn't possible here even though 'url' is tainted (the real risk is SSRF via fetch(url), a different CWE class).
User-controlled req.query.q (only length-limited, not escaped/parameterized) is directly interpolated into a raw SQL string executed via sequelize.query, allowing classic UNION-based SQL injection.

The 'sink' is security.authenticatedUsers.get(req.cookies.token), a lookup in an in-memory session map, not a network request; there is no outbound HTTP call using tainted data, so this cannot be SSRF. The tool mis-classified a simple map lookup as an SSRF sink.

process.env.BASE_PATH is a server-side configuration value set by the deployment operator, not attacker-controlled input reachable via HTTP request; treating process.env as a generic taint source misclassifies trusted server config as tainted data.

req.cookies.token is only used as a key into an in-memory Map (security.authenticatedUsers.get), not passed to any network request or SSRF sink; this is a mis-tagged finding with no outbound request present.

`key` comes directly from `req.body.key` (untrusted user input) and is concatenated unsanitized into a filesystem path passed to `fs.existsSync`/`fs.readFileSync`, allowing path traversal (e.g. `key=../../../etc/passwd%00` or similar) to read arbitrary `.info.yml`-suffixed files outside the intended directory.

The tainted 'key' only influences which local .yml filename is read from a fixed codefixes directory (potential path traversal, CWE-22), not the YAML content passed to yaml.load; since js-yaml's load() in v4 uses the safe default schema and the deserialized data itself is not attacker-controlled, there's no exploitable deserialization sink here.

`key` is used to index into a persistent, module-scoped `CodeFixes` cache object with no allowlist/sanitization shown, and appears to originate directly from a request parameter (per the `FixesRequestParams`/`VerdictRequestBody` interfaces below). If an attacker supplies key="__proto__", `CodeFixes[key] = {...}` reassigns CodeFixes's own prototype link, corrupting a shared server-side object (potential DoS/behavior break for all subsequent requests) — a narrower but still real instance of the un

The regex /_(\d+)/ is a simple linear pattern with a single non-nested quantifier and no overlapping alternations or ambiguous repetition, so it cannot exhibit catastrophic backtracking regardless of input; this is a mis-classification by the tool.

This is a test fixture in a *.test.ts file creating a mock user account for a 'nonexistent user' test case; the literal '123456' is dummy test data, not a real credential guarding any production system.

This is a test fixture in a *.test.ts file where a fake TOTP secret is used to construct a test case for a nonexistent user; it is not a credential protecting any real system or reachable from untrusted input.

This is a unit test file assigning a literal JWT string to a mocked request object's cookie field purely to set up test fixtures for retrieveLoggedInUser(); it is not a credential embedded in production code or reachable from untrusted input.

This is unit test code that temporarily sets process.env.ALCHEMY_API_KEY to a dummy 'test-key' string to test checkIfEnvironmentVariableExists(), restoring the original value in an after() hook; it is not a real credential and not reachable in production code.
